name: Lottie Deep-Dive — Creation + Optimization + Runtime
definition_origin: Lottie is a JSON-based vector animation format originally created by Hernan Torrisi (Bodymovin plugin for After Effects) and open-sourced by Airbnb in 2017. It encodes After Effects compositions as a JSON tree of layers, shapes, and keyframes that runtime libraries (lottie-web, lottie-ios, lottie-android, lottie-react-native) interpret and render natively. dotLottie (.lottie) is the 2023 sibling format: a ZIP container that bundles Lottie JSON + assets (images, fonts) and applies gzip compression — typically 50-80% smaller than raw .json.
core_purpose: Lottie's job is to deliver brand-quality character + UI animation at vector quality with file sizes 6-10x smaller than equivalent GIF or MP4. Visceral level: the playful bounce of Duolingo's owl, the celebratory confetti after a goal, the breathing meditation circle. Behavioral level: loading spinners, success checkmarks, error shakes — animations that confirm user action. Reflective level: brand-defining motion (Airbnb's rebooking, Stripe's payment success) that becomes part of product memory. Lottie excels when motion is LINEAR (plays start-to-end without branching), and it falters when you need true interactivity (state machines were bolted on later and remain limited compared to Rive).
when_needed: Consumer mobile: empty states, onboarding mascots, celebratory moments, micro-success animations. B2B/dev-tool: minimal — empty-state illustrations, deploy-success confetti. AI-wrapper: 'thinking' / loading animations during model inference. Marketing surfaces: hero illustrations on landing pages, step-by-step explainer animations.
format_options: Two file formats: (1) **Lottie JSON (.json)** — human-readable, larger, depends on external assets if any are referenced; widely supported by every runtime. (2) **dotLottie (.lottie)** — ZIP container with gzip compression, bundles assets, 50-80% smaller. dotLottie is the 2026 default for production. Rendering targets: SVG renderer (default lottie-web, broadest compat, slower CPU-bound), Canvas renderer (better perf for many concurrent animations), HTML renderer (rare). On native: lottie-ios uses Core Animation, lottie-android uses canvas; lottie-react-native bridges both. Animation can be: full playback (autoplay loop), segmented (play frames 0-30 only), speed-controlled, paused/scrubbed manually, or driven by state machine inputs (newer feature, limited).
file_size_performance: Target: <50KB for icon-sized animation, <100KB for character animation, <200KB for full-screen onboarding. dotLottie shaves 50-80% off raw JSON. Optimization order: (1) reduce keyframes via Lottielab Keyframe Optimizer or LottieFiles Advanced Optimizer (often 30-50% reduction); (2) simplify bezier paths (remove redundant points); (3) reduce decimal precision (125.4326 → 125.43, ~5-10% saving); (4) externalize or compress embedded images (often biggest win); (5) wrap in dotLottie container. Runtime cost: lottie-web SVG renderer is CPU-bound — 3+ concurrent on a low-end Android can hit 17fps. Switching to Canvas renderer or Skottie (Skia-based) helps. lottie-react-native uses native Lottie libs which are faster than the web equivalent.
free_tools: 1) **Lottielab** (lottielab.com) — free web-based Lottie editor, no After Effects required. Build animations in-browser, export Lottie/dotLottie, supports importing existing Lotties. Free tier: limited project count [uncertain]. 2) **LottieFiles** (lottiefiles.com) — free animation library (browse + download), free Bodymovin-equivalent After Effects plugin, free LottieFiles Optimizer + Advanced Optimizer (web). 3) **Bodymovin** (github.com/airbnb/lottie-web) — free After Effects plugin, requires AE itself (paid), but the plugin is free. 4) **TinyLottie** (tiny-lottie.vercel.app) — free Lottie/dotLottie optimizer, drag-and-drop. 5) **Lottiemizer** (lottiemizer.com) — free Lottie size reduction tool. 6) **Jitter** (jitter.video) — free tier motion design web tool, exports Lottie. 7) **Haiku Animator** — free tier, more limited. 8) **Phase** (phase.com) — design-tool-style Lottie editor, free tier [uncertain]. 9) **dotLottie tools** (lottietools.app) — free JSON↔.lottie converter.
paid_tools: Starter $10-15/mo: **LottieFiles Pro** (~$10/mo individual [uncertain]) — collaboration, larger storage, priority optimizer. Pro $20-30/mo: **Adobe After Effects single-app** (~$23/mo) — gold standard for complex character animation; Bodymovin plugin is free but requires AE. Pro $20-30/mo: **Lottielab Pro** (~$20/mo [uncertain]) — unlimited projects, advanced features. Enterprise $50+/mo: **LottieFiles Org Plan** — team workspace, brand kit, SSO. For a solo broke dev: free Lottielab + free LottieFiles library covers 90% of needs. Only justify AE+Bodymovin if you're doing heavy character animation and already know AE.
gemini_pro_workflow: Gemini does not output Lottie directly, but accelerates the upstream art. WORKFLOW: 1) Use **Imagen 4** to generate static keyframe poses of your character or icon (e.g. 'flat-design owl mascot, pose 1: idle waving; pose 2: celebrating with arms up; pose 3: thinking with hand on chin. Identical proportions, 5-color palette #58CC02 #FFC800 #1CB0F6 #FFFFFF #000000, transparent background.'). 2) Bring the static art into Lottielab — trace/reconstruct as vectors using Lottielab's pen tool, or import SVGs vectorized from the Imagen output. 3) Build the timeline animation in Lottielab. 4) Use **Gems** to build a 'Lottie Brand Motion Gem' loaded with this research + your brand bible — ask the Gem 'what easing curve and timing should I use for a celebratory animation?' or 'what's the right loop length for a meditation breathing animation?' 5) Use **NotebookLM** to load lottie-react-native docs, dotLottie spec, and your brand guide as sources, then ask 'how do I animate the success checkmark to feel friendly but not childish?' GEMINI PRO PROMPT for Imagen 4 keyframes: 'Three pose keyframes of [character], identical lighting and proportions, [brand palette hex codes], white background, suitable for tracing into vector animation. Pose 1: [state]. Pose 2: [transition]. Pose 3: [end state].' Veo can generate reference video for animation timing, but its output is MP4, not Lottie — useful as motion reference only.
workflow_steps: 1) Decide if Lottie is the right format (linear motion, no complex interactivity) — 5 min. 2) Sketch keyframes on paper or generate in Imagen 4 — 15 min. 3) Open Lottielab (free, browser-based), create new project at target dimensions (typically 200x200 for icon, 1080x1080 for hero) — 5 min. 4) Build vector shapes in Lottielab using pen + shape tools, or import SVG layers — 30-60 min. 5) Set keyframes on timeline: position, scale, rotation, opacity, path morphs — 30-90 min. 6) Apply easing (Lottielab presets — ease-in-out, bounce, elastic) — 10 min. 7) Preview at multiple speeds, check on mobile-sized canvas — 10 min. 8) Export as dotLottie (.lottie) — 1 min. 9) Run through TinyLottie or LottieFiles Optimizer for additional 30-50% reduction — 5 min. 10) Drop into apps/mobile/src/assets/lottie/ and integrate via lottie-react-native LottieView — 10 min. Total: ~2-4 hours per moderate animation for a beginner.
ai_prompt_templates: 1) **Imagen 4 keyframe set prompt**: 'A clean flat-design vector illustration set of 3 sequential poses of {character_description}: frame 1 = {pose_state}, frame 2 = {pose_state}, frame 3 = {pose_state}. Identical proportions and color palette {hex_list}. Hard edges, no gradients, transparent background, suitable for vectorization into a Lottie animation. Style reference: Duolingo, Headspace.' 2) **Gem prompt for Lottie planning**: 'I want to build a Lottie animation for {use_case} matching {brand_archetype} brand. Recommend: duration in seconds, loop type (one-shot vs. loop), easing function, key motion verbs (bounce/glide/snap), and the file-size budget. Anti-slop guardrails to apply.' 3) **NotebookLM brand-bible query**: 'Given my brand voice [Caregiver / Magician / Jester / Sage], what motion principles match? Reference Duolingo for Jester, Headspace for Caregiver.' 4) **Lottielab task prompt** (after generating concept): 'Build a 60-frame loop at 30fps, 200x200 canvas, of {described_motion} with ease-in-out 0.4 / 0.2 / 0.6 / 1.0 cubic bezier.'
claude_code_integration: Install: `pnpm add lottie-react-native@^7.3.6` (latest stable as of Feb 2026). For dotLottie specifically: `pnpm add @lottiefiles/dotlottie-react-native` (or use lottie-react-native 7+ which supports both). Drop assets in `apps/mobile/src/assets/lottie/` (e.g. `success.lottie`, `loading.lottie`). Wrapper component pattern: `<LottieView source={require('@/assets/lottie/success.lottie')} autoPlay loop={false} style={{width: 200, height: 200}} />`. For prefers-reduced-motion: `import { useReducedMotion } from '@/hooks/useReducedMotion'` and conditionally render a static frame. For programmatic control: `const ref = useRef<LottieView>(null); ref.current?.play(0, 30);`. Optimization in CI: add `scripts/optimize-lottie.ts` that runs LottieFiles CLI optimizer on every .lottie in the assets folder. Claude Code can scaffold a `<Lottie>` wrapper with reduced-motion handling baked in.
emotional_design_integration: Caregiver: slow ease-in-out, 1-2s loops, gentle scale + opacity (Headspace breathing). Avoid sharp bounces. Magician: morphing path animations (Stripe's payment success — checkmark draws on with anticipation). Easing: ease-out with slight overshoot. Jester: bounce curves, exaggerated scale (Duolingo's owl celebrating with 1.2x scale + rotation). Hero: fast snappy success animations (1s, ease-out). Sage: minimal motion, only when functional. Outlaw: glitchy easing, off-rhythm timing. The same checkmark animation morphs identity by easing curve alone — same paths, very different feel.
specific_examples: 1) **Duolingo** — Lottie-heavy: owl mascot celebrations, streak fire animations, lesson-complete confetti. Why it works: animations are short (1-2s), color-disciplined to brand palette, character has consistent proportions across all animations. https://blog.duolingo.com/shape-language-duolingos-art-style/ 2) **Headspace** — breathing circle Lottie that pulses for the meditation timer; slow loops, soft opacity changes. Why it works: 4-second cycle matches breath rhythm, no harsh easing, loops perfectly. 3) **Airbnb (lottie-web origin)** — rebooking-success animation, illustrative travel scenes in onboarding. https://airbnb.io/lottie 4) **Stripe Dashboard** — payment-success checkmark Lottie + 'no transactions yet' empty-state Lottie. Restraint is the point: only used when functional. 5) **Notion** — Lottie empty states (e.g. 'no pages yet' bird character). 6) **LottieFiles community page** itself shows production examples: https://lottiefiles.com/featured.
anti_ai_slop_traps: Lottie-specific slop tells: (a) **'Trending on LottieFiles' clones** — every solo dev grabs the same gradient-blob loading spinner from the free library. Counter: even a hand-drawn squiggle in Lottielab is more distinctive; build one custom asset matching brand palette. (b) **Off-brand colors** — downloaded Lottie has purple/pink default colors; ship without recoloring. Counter: LottieFiles has a built-in color editor, OR open the JSON and find/replace hex codes. (c) **Generic 'isometric people'** illustrations from LottieFiles browse — instant SaaS-slop tell. Counter: avoid isometric entirely; use frontal flat-design. (d) **Over-animated UI** — every button is a Lottie. Counter: Lottie is for moments (success, error, empty state, loading), not every interaction. (e) **Auto-traced from photo** — Imagen 4 photo run through tracer creates 5000-point bezier monstrosity. Counter: keep upstream art flat-design with hard edges, vectorize manually with simplification. EXPLICIT NEGATIVE PROMPT for Imagen 4 source art: 'no isometric, no gradient mesh, no 3D perspective, no chibi, no generic SaaS purple-blue, no melted hands, no random floating shapes.'
common_pitfalls: 1) Embedding huge raster images in Lottie JSON — kills the size advantage; always externalize or use dotLottie which compresses them. 2) Not testing on low-end Android — 200KB Lottie can hit 12fps on an A20. 3) Over-using Lottie where CSS-animated SVG would do — a button hover doesn't need Lottie. 4) Inconsistent Lottie style across the app — one file in Lottielab style, one downloaded from LottieFiles, one from After Effects = visual chaos. 5) Forgetting to set loop={false} on success animations — checkmark loops forever, distracting. 6) Not respecting prefers-reduced-motion — accessibility fail. 7) Auto-playing Lotties on screens that aren't visible — burns CPU and battery; use IntersectionObserver or lazy-mount.
accessibility_notes: Provide aria-label on the container ('Loading' / 'Success'). Respect prefers-reduced-motion: render a static first-or-last frame instead of animating. lottie-react-native: pass speed=0 and progress=1 to freeze on final frame. For Lotties that convey meaning (e.g. 'error'), do not rely on animation alone — pair with visible text. Avoid flashing animations that could trigger photosensitive epilepsy (no >3 flashes/sec).
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile (RN/Expo): lottie-react-native@7.3.6 wraps native iOS/Android Lottie libs, GPU-accelerated, supports both .json and .lottie. Web: lottie-web (SVG/Canvas/HTML renderer), or @lottiefiles/dotlottie-web for .lottie. Web is more CPU-bound; use Canvas renderer when running 3+ concurrent animations. Bundle .lottie in mobile assets directory or load via OTA / CDN; on web, lazy-load via dynamic import to avoid blocking initial render.
sources: 1) [airbnb/lottie-web](https://github.com/airbnb/lottie-web) — original repo. 2) [lottie-react-native](https://github.com/lottie-react-native/lottie-react-native) — RN runtime. 3) [dotLottie spec](https://dotlottie.io/intro/) — official .lottie format docs. 4) [LottieFiles Optimize Guide](https://lottiefiles.com/blog/working-with-lottie-animations/optimize-lottie-files-for-faster-page-load-speeds). 5) [Lottielab](https://www.lottielab.com/) — free editor. 6) [LottieFiles for After Effects](https://lottiefiles.com/plugins/after-effects). 7) [Duolingo shape language](https://blog.duolingo.com/shape-language-duolingos-art-style/) — production example. 8) [TinyLottie](https://tiny-lottie.vercel.app/) — free optimizer.
